verses.text: The people recall how God, not their fathers' own sword, won them the land, for He favoured them. They rehearse old mercies. Remembering past grace is the right footing for present prayer.
The people recall how God, not their fathers' own sword, won them the land, for He favoured them. They rehearse old mercies. Remembering past grace is the right footing for present prayer.
The people recall how God, not their fathers' own sword, won them the land, for He favoured them. They rehearse old mercies. Remembering past grace is the right footing for present prayer.

'Thou art my King, O God,' they confess; through Him they push down foes and in God they boast all day, not trusting their own bow. Victory is owned as His. The people of God glory not in their strength but in their King.

'Thou art my King, O God,' they confess; through Him they push down foes and in God they boast all day, not trusting their own bow. Victory is owned as His. The people of God glory not in their strength but in their King.

'Thou art my King, O God,' they confess; through Him they push down foes and in God they boast all day, not trusting their own bow. Victory is owned as His. The people of God glory not in their strength but in their King.
Yet now, they lament, God has cast them off, made them retreat, and 'sold' them cheaply. Defeat bewilders them. There are dark seasons when God's people feel abandoned despite their faithfulness.

Yet now, they lament, God has cast them off, made them retreat, and 'sold' them cheaply. Defeat bewilders them. There are dark seasons when God's people feel abandoned despite their faithfulness.
They have become a reproach and byword among the nations, covered with shame. Public scorn weighs on them. The afflicted church may bear contempt it has not deserved.
They have become a reproach and byword among the nations, covered with shame. Public scorn weighs on them. The afflicted church may bear contempt it has not deserved.

'All this is come upon us; yet have we not forgotten thee,' they protest, having kept the covenant though crushed. They suffer without conscious cause. Not all affliction is punishment; some is mystery to be borne in faith.

'All this is come upon us; yet have we not forgotten thee,' they protest, having kept the covenant though crushed. They suffer without conscious cause. Not all affliction is punishment; some is mystery to be borne in faith.

'All this is come upon us; yet have we not forgotten thee,' they protest, having kept the covenant though crushed. They suffer without conscious cause. Not all affliction is punishment; some is mystery to be borne in faith.
Had they forgotten God for an idol, He would have known; instead, 'for thy sake are we killed all the day long.' They suffer for God's name. Paul cites this of the persecuted church in every age.

'Awake, why sleepest thou, O Lord?' they cry; 'arise for our help, and redeem us for thy mercies' sake.' They appeal not to merit but to mercy. When all else fails, God's lovingkindness is the last and surest plea.
